name: Men Shen
description: Men Shen, literally translated as 'Door Gods,' are a pair of divine guardians of doors and entryways in Chinese folk religion. They are traditionally depicted as two imposing figures, often Tang dynasty generals, whose images are affixed to doors to ward off evil spirits and invite good fortune into the household. Their origins are rooted in a complex blend of ancestor veneration, folk beliefs, and imperial decree. The practice of posting images of Men Shen is believed to have originated during the Tang Dynasty, with various historical figures being associated with the role, including Qin Shubao and Yuchi Gong. These figures are often portrayed in full military regalia, armed and ready to defend the inhabitants within. Their presence symbolizes protection, security, and the maintenance of order against chaotic supernatural forces. The specific iconography and the figures represented can vary regionally, reflecting the diverse cultural landscape of China. They represent a powerful manifestation of the Chinese belief in the protective power of deities and the importance of securing the home against malevolent influences.
subtitle: Guardians of the Gate

keyMyths.title: The Sickness of Emperor Taizong
keyMyths.description: Emperor Taizong of the Tang Dynasty was plagued by nightmares and illness. He believed that evil spirits were tormenting him at night. His generals, Qin Shubao and Yuchi Gong, volunteered to stand guard at his door. Their fierce appearance and powerful energy successfully scared away the demons, allowing the Emperor to sleep peacefully. To alleviate the generals' burden, the Emperor ordered their portraits to be painted and affixed to the palace gates, thus establishing the tradition of Men Shen.
keyMyths.source: Werner, E. T. C. Myths and Legends of China.

keyMyths.title: Origin from Shen Tu and Yu Lei
keyMyths.description: Another origin story links the Men Shen to the earlier figures of Shen Tu and Yu Lei, two mythical brothers who were skilled at capturing demons. They were said to have lived on a peach tree in Dushuo Mountain, and they would tie up any evil spirits they caught with reeds. Eventually, their images were painted on doors as a form of protection, evolving into the later Men Shen figures. This origin highlights the ancient roots of protective deities in Chinese folklore.
keyMyths.source: Williams, C. A. S. Chinese Symbolism and Art Motives.

keyMyths.title: The Loyal Generals
keyMyths.description: Qin Shubao and Yuchi Gong were not just skilled warriors; they were also renowned for their unwavering loyalty to the Emperor. This loyalty became a crucial aspect of their role as Men Shen. Their willingness to sacrifice their comfort and even their lives to protect the Emperor resonated deeply with the common people, solidifying their position as symbols of steadfastness and protection. Their images on the door served as a constant reminder of the importance of loyalty and dedication.
keyMyths.source: Eberhard, Wolfram. A Dictionary of Chinese Symbols: Hidden Symbols in Chinese Life and Thought.

sources.author: Werner, E. T. C.
